title: The stop cut
subtitle: Setting a boundary before relief and shaping cuts
excerpt: A stop cut is a short vertical cut into the wood that prevents the next push or paring cut from running past the intended stopping point. It defines the boundary first, so the following cut can be made confidently. Used in chip carving, relief work, and any situation where a cut must stop at a precise edge.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for stop-cut descriptions in carving and relief work.

glossaryTerms.definition: A short vertical cut made perpendicular to the wood surface that sets a boundary for subsequent cuts. The following cut runs up to the stop cut and breaks out cleanly at the boundary rather than tearing past it.
Three converging cuts that lift a small triangular chip from the surface. The chip-carving fundamental, always preceded by a stop cut at each angle.

content.text: The
stop cut

is a single vertical cut that defines where a subsequent cut must end. Without it, a push cut or paring cut travelling along the grain will run past the intended stopping point, driven by the wood grain rather than the carver's intention. The stop cut severs the grain fibres at the boundary first, so the following cut breaks out cleanly at the defined edge rather than tearing past it.
When to use it

Use the stop cut whenever a carving cut must stop at a defined edge and the grain direction could carry the blade past that edge. The three most common situations are: chip carving patterns (where each triangle is defined by three stop cuts before any material is removed), relief carving (where an outline cut defines the panel boundary before the ground is lowered around it), and making a shoulder cut in any woodwork where two surfaces meet at a corner.
What you need

A sloyd knife for general stop cuts. A detail knife for very precise chip-carving stop cuts where the tip needs to enter a marked corner cleanly. For practice, a piece of flat seasoned lime or basswood is ideal: the grain is even and the wood is soft enough to be forgiving of technique.
The technique
Step 1: Mark or identify the boundary

The stop cut begins with a clear line to follow. In chip carving this is a pencilled triangle. In relief work it is a drawn outline. In joinery it is a scribed line from a marking knife or gauge. The line must be visible and precisely where you intend the final boundary to sit.
Step 2: Make the stop cut

Place the tip of the knife on the marked line, blade perpendicular to the wood surface (90 degrees, straight down). Press firmly and vertically into the wood to a depth of 2 to 3 mm for chip carving, or to the full depth of the shoulder for joinery work. The cut severs the grain fibres at the boundary line. Do not angle the blade forward or backward; a stop cut that leans even slightly will leave a sloped wall where a vertical one is needed.
Step 3: Make the following cut

Now make the shaping cut that will run up to the stop cut. This is typically a push cut or thumb-pivot cut directed toward the stop cut line, slightly below the surface. As the shaping cut reaches the stop cut, it breaks out at the boundary line rather than tearing past it. The chip lifts cleanly away. On dense or dry wood, the stop cut may need to be deepened slightly if the shaping cut still tears through; a second pass of the stop cut to full depth resolves this.
The chip cut
The
chip cut

is the direct application of the stop cut in chip carving. Mark a triangle on the surface. Make a stop cut along each of the three sides, angling the blade slightly inward toward the centre of the triangle on each cut (this creates the V-shaped groove that will frame the lifted chip). Then make three shaping cuts from the corners toward the centre of the triangle, below the surface, angled toward a common meeting point in the middle. If all three are at the same depth and angle, the chip lifts as one clean piece. If they do not meet at the same depth, the chip breaks partway.
What it looks like when it is right

A clean stop cut followed by a clean shaping cut produces a sharp, crisp boundary. The corner or edge is a clean line rather than a torn, ragged one. In chip carving, the three cuts converge and lift the chip as a single piece, leaving a faceted triangular depression with flat walls. If the walls are torn or the boundary is ragged, either the stop cut did not go deep enough, the knife was angled rather than vertical, or the wood was cut against its grain.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: What goes wrong with stop cuts and how to fix it.

items.symptom: The following cut tears past the stop-cut line
items.cause: The stop cut was not deep enough to sever the grain fibres fully at the boundary.
items.fix: Deepen the stop cut with a second vertical press, then repeat the shaping cut.

items.symptom: The chip breaks rather than lifting as one piece
items.cause: In a three-cut chip, the shaping cuts did not meet at the same depth or angle in the centre.
items.fix: Make the shaping cuts more deliberately, holding the same blade angle throughout each cut and checking that all three are meeting at approximately the same depth.

items.symptom: The stop cut walls are sloped rather than vertical
items.cause: The blade was angled forward or backward during the stop cut rather than held straight down.
items.fix: Recheck blade angle before pressing. For very tight chip-carving triangles, rest the blade tip on the marked corner and look down the blade to confirm it is perpendicular before pressing.
